sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 76, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Starting level 1. The board shows several colored objects on a gray background with a pink bar at the bottom (likely a HUD/timer). Need to identify objects and their relationships.
- Plan: First, inspect the segmentation to understand what objects exist, then probe actions to understand the game mechanics.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 75 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The board didn't change! The player couldn't move UP even with W collapsed. Let me think about what's different between the right frame collection and the left frame situation.
Looking at the right frame collection:
- Player was at row 32-35, right frame at row 24-27
- Player moved UP to row 28-31, frame was collected
- The player's W part was 4 pixels (collapsed) at [[32, 47], [35, 47]]
- The player's N part was at [[32, 44], [32, 46], [35, 46], [35, 44]]
- The right frame was at [[24, 44], [24, 47], [27, 47], [27, 44]]
- The player and frame shared the same column range (44-47)
For the left frame:
- Player is at row 36-39, left frame at row 28-31
- Player's W part is 4 pixels (collapsed) at [[36, 16], [36, 19]]
- Player's N part is at [[37, 16], [37, 19], [39, 19], [39, 16]]
- Left frame is at [[28, 16], [28, 19], [31, 19], [31, 16]]
- The player and frame share the same column range (16-19)
The key difference might be the blue square at [[33, 17], ...]. This blue square is between the player and the left frame. It's at row 33-34, which is between the player (row 36-39) and the left frame (row 28-31).
When the right frame was collected, was there a blue square between the player and the frame? Looking at the step 20 state:
- Player at row 32-35, right frame at row 24-27
- Blue squares at [[25, 45], ...] (inside right frame), [[29, 17], ...] (inside left frame), [[37, 33], ...] (inside bottom frame)
- No blue square between player and right frame
So the blue square at [[33, 17], ...] is blocking the player's movement UP. This blue square appeared after the SPACE action. It's attached to the player (moves with the player).
Maybe the blue square is a "projectile" that needs to hit the frame to collect it. But the blue square is at row 33-34, and the left frame is at row 28-31. The blue square is not inside the frame.
Wait, maybe the blue square needs to be INSIDE the frame to collect it. The blue square at [[29, 17], ...] is already inside the left frame. So maybe the left frame is already "collected" in some sense, but it hasn't disappeared yet.
Or maybe the blue square at [[33, 17], ...] is blocking the player, and I need to remove it first.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe I need to move the player to a different column, then move UP, then move back to collect the frame.
Actually, let me look at the blue square at [[33, 17], ...] more carefully. It appeared after the SPACE action at step 61 -> 62. Before that, the player didn't have a blue square attached.
Looking at the step 60 state (before SPACE):
- Player W: 16 pixels at [[32, 16], [32, 19], [36, 19], [36, 16]]
- Player N: 12 pixels at [[37, 16], [37, 19], [39, 19], [39, 16]]
- No blue square at [[33, 17], ...]
After SPACE at step 61 -> 62:
- Player W: 4 pixels at [[36, 16], [36, 19]]
- Player N: 12 pixels at [[37, 16], [37, 19], [39, 19], [39, 16]]
- Blue square at [[33, 17], ...] appeared
So SPACE created a blue square at [[33, 17], ...], which is inside the player's W part area (rows 32-36, cols 16-19). This blue square is blocking the player's movement UP.
Maybe I need to press SPACE again to remove the blue square? Or maybe the blue square is a "key" that needs to be placed in the frame.
Actually, looking at the left frame at [[28, 16], ...], it already has a blue square at [[29, 17], ...] inside it. So the left frame already has a blue square. Maybe the blue square at [[33, 17], ...] is a duplicate that was created by SPACE, and it's blocking the player.
Let me try pressing SPACE again to see if it removes the blue square.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Blue square at [[33, 17], ...] is blocking player's UP movement
# Try pressing SPACE to toggle/remove the blue square
action(['SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
